name: Pediatrics: The Child with a Skin Condition

scenarios.question: Which statement best describes Chilblain?
options: This is not a recognized term in pediatric nursing
This is an outdated concept no longer taught
a cold injury with erythema and formation of vesicles and ulcerative lesions tha...
This only applies to adult patients, not children
scenarios.rationale: Chilblain: a cold injury with erythema and formation of vesicles and ulcerative lesions that occur as a result of vasoconstriction In exposure to extreme cold, w (Slide 34)

scenarios.question: When caring for pediatric patients, the nurse should:
options: Use the same approach as with adult patients
Exclude family members from care decisions
Focus only on the child's physical symptoms
be warmed before the extremities to ensure survival
scenarios.rationale: be warmed before the extremities to ensure survival. Family-centered, age-appropriate care is essential in pediatrics. (Slide 34)

scenarios.question: Which of the following are expected findings in The Child with a Skin Condition? (Select all that apply.)
options: of a systemic disease; others may be congenital, Skin color
Findings opposite to what was described
scenarios.rationale: of a systemic disease; others may be congenital, Skin color  is a characteristic finding. Skin Disorders and Variations Skin condition may be a manifestation of a systemi... (Slide 4)

scenarios.question: What is the primary treatment approach for this pediatric condition?
options: is either oral or parenteral antibiotics 18
scenarios.rationale: is either oral or parenteral antibiotics 18. Impetigo Caused by staphylococci or group A beta-hemolytic streptococci Bullous ... (Slide 18)

scenarios.question: Atopic dermatitis (eczema) is characterized by:
options: Pruritic, dry, scaly patches in flexural areas
Linear lesions in a dermatomal pattern
scenarios.rationale: Atopic dermatitis: chronic, relapsing, itchy; flexural in older children; cheeks/scalp in infants. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: What is the primary treatment for eczema?
scenarios.rationale: Emollients (moisturizers) are cornerstone. Topical steroids for flares. Avoid triggers (soap, wool). (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Impetigo is caused by:
scenarios.rationale: Impetigo: bacterial (staph/strep); honey-colored crusts around nose/mouth. Highly contagious. Topical/oral antibiotics. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Contact with poison ivy causes:
options: Allergic contact dermatitis with linear vesicles
scenarios.rationale: Poison ivy/oak/sumac: urushiol oil causes allergic reaction. Linear vesicles from plant contact. Wash skin immediately. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: What is the first-line treatment for head lice?
scenarios.rationale: Permethrin 1% (Nix): apply to damp hair, leave 10 min, rinse. Repeat in 7-10 days. Comb out nits. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Scabies is caused by:
scenarios.rationale: Scabies: mite burrows into skin → intensely itchy, especially at night. Linear burrows, papules. Permethrin cream. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: A child with a burn covering 15% of body surface area:
scenarios.rationale: Burns >10% BSA in children require hospitalization. Assess depth (partial vs full thickness). (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: What is the Rule of Nines in children?
options: Modified to account for larger head and smaller legs
scenarios.rationale: Pediatric Rule of Nines: head=18%, each leg=14%, trunk=36%. Palm method for small burns. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: What is the most important intervention for a child with a burn?
options: Stop the burning process and ensure airway
scenarios.rationale: Stop burning (remove clothing, cool water), ABCs, assess extent/depth. Do NOT apply ice or butter. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: A child with a wound that has honey-colored crusts most likely has:
scenarios.rationale: Honey-colored crusts = impetigo (staph/strep). Highly contagious. Treat with antibiotics. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Diaper rash (candidal) presents with:
options: Bright red rash with satellite lesions in skin folds
scenarios.rationale: Candida diaper rash: bright red, satellite lesions. Treat with antifungal (nystatin). (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Atopic dermatitis (eczema) is associated with:
scenarios.rationale: Atopic triad: eczema, asthma, allergic rhinitis. Family history common. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: For eczema, emollients should be applied:
options: Immediately after bathing while skin is damp
scenarios.rationale: Emollients: apply within 3 minutes of bathing on damp skin. Prevents water loss. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Impetigo is highly contagious. Children should:
options: Stay home until 24h after antibiotics started
scenarios.rationale: Impetigo: exclude from school until 24h after antibiotics. Cover lesions. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Burn depth: partial thickness (2nd degree) involves:
options: Only the top layer of dermis
scenarios.rationale: Partial thickness: epidermis + dermis. Blisters, pink/red, painful. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Full thickness burns (3rd degree) appear:
scenarios.rationale: Full thickness: white/charred, leathery, painless (nerve destruction). (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: For a burn, cool the area with:
scenarios.rationale: Cool water (not ice) for 10-20 min. Ice causes more tissue damage. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Sunburn prevention for children includes:
scenarios.rationale: Sun protection: SPF 30+ sunscreen, reapply every 2h, hats, shade. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Seborrheic dermatitis (cradle cap) in infants:
scenarios.rationale: Cradle cap: greasy yellow scales on scalp. Treat with mineral oil, brush gently. (Standard NCLEX)
